data.title: US public backs active role in global affairs, including Taiwan defence, poll finds
data.description:
The American public still wants the United States to be “engaged and take the lead” in international affairs, with support increasing for military intervention if Taiwan were to be attacked, according to a new survey.
This view contrasts with US President Donald Trump’s isolationist bent and pivot towards homeland security, something analysts say could “complicate” decision making in the event of a crisis in the Taiwan Strait.
According to an annual survey published on Thursday by the Ronald Reagan Institute, a US non-profit organisation created by the former US president, 64 per cent of Americans, a “record high” in the eight years of polling, said the US should be “more engaged and take the lead in international affairs”, in contrast to 33 per cent who preferred less engagement.
Nearly nine out of 10 Americans questioned – 87 per cent – also said it was important that the US had the most powerful military in the world. Meanwhile, 71 per cent agreed that the world was more likely to achieve peace when the US was the strongest power, while 44 per cent wanted a military capable of “defeating China and Russia simultaneously”.
China topped the list of countries Americans saw as the greatest threat to the US, accounting for 48 per cent of the replies, followed by Russia on 26 per cent.
The survey report said Americans’ support for defending Taiwan was “strong, bipartisan and growing”, with 77 per cent saying it would be important for the US military to defend Taiwan if it was attacked by mainland China.
The poll also found that if that happened, 60 per cent said they would support committing US forces to Taipei’s defence – up from 48 per cent last year – while 70 per cent supported moving US assets to the region and 79 per cent now backed official recognition of the island as independent, all edging up from the previous survey.
The survey was conducted from October 23 to November 3, with a sample of 2,507 respondents.
Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China and has never ruled out the use of force to reunite it with the mainland. Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the island by force and is committed to supplying it with arms.
Washington has maintained a policy of strategic ambiguity about its role if Beijing were to attack the self-ruled island. Former US president Joe Biden had explicitly stated that the US would defend Taiwan from a People’s Liberation Army attack, but such claims were always followed by clarification that US policy remained unchanged. Trump’s stance on the issue has been less clear.
The survey also found that favourability towards Nato had reached its highest level ever at 68 per cent, with strong bipartisan support for Article 5, which states that an attack on one member is an attack on all.


A strong majority of 62 per cent wanted Ukraine to prevail in its war with Russia, and 64 per cent supported sending US weapons – up nine points from last year.
The results were released ahead of a defence forum the institute is hosting in California on Saturday, which will be attended by Trump administration officials, lawmakers and defence contractors.
Benjamin Barton, an associate professor of international relations at the University of Nottingham Malaysia, said the timing of the release was “hardly inconspicuous” when elements of the Republican Party were concerned about the “electoral risk” of being too closely associated with Trump.
“The Reagan institute has had a rocky relationship with the second Trump presidency … this feels to me as if the Reagan institute is also seizing the moment to advance a position which clearly is designed to criticise and castigate the incumbent administration, whilst currying favour with more traditional branches of the Republican Party,” Barton said.
“The Trump administration would likely take offence at the findings of this survey and instead of paying heed to them, it would double down on a policy of confronting naysayers – especially those tied to the [Republican Party].”
On Friday, the White House published the new National Security Strategy that outlined its strategic priorities. Compared with previous documents, the document placed more emphasis on issues such as domestic security and the western hemisphere, but China still played a significant role.
The release of the documents was delayed for several weeks because Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent wanted to soften the language on China, given the sensitivity surrounding ongoing trade talks, according to a report by Politico, which cited sources familiar with the discussions.
The National Security Strategy is updated by every administration. The previous version, published under Biden in 2022, focused on issues such as strategic competition with China and Russia, climate change and domestic investment and industrial policy.
Liselotte Odgaard, a senior fellow at the Hudson Institute in Washington, said increased support for international engagement did not “eliminate Trump’s ability to pursue western hemisphere security priorities, but it likely constrains the administration from adopting purely unilateral or militarised strategies”.
“With public support for defending Taiwan at historic highs, Congress and defence stakeholders may push back against any perceived abandonment of Taiwan,” she added.
“If Trump signals hesitation [or demands increased defence spending] it risks undermining deterrence and emboldening Beijing. Public opinion favouring intervention could amplify criticism of such moves, creating domestic political costs.
“The gap between public opinion and Trump’s ‘America first’ policy could delay or complicate decision making in a Taiwan crisis and increase the domestic costs of non-intervention, pushing Trump towards some kind of a conditional intervention.”
Washington has already increased its military presence in the Caribbean and eastern Pacific, carrying out strikes on ships that it says were carrying drugs.
Trump has accused Venezuela of being behind the traffickers and said on Thursday that military strikes on targets inside the country would begin “very soon”. He is also reported to have given Venezuelan President Nicolas Maduro an ultimatum to leave the country.
Amid rumours of an invasion, a group of US senators have filed a war powers resolution that would require congressional approval to use force against Venezuela.
Odgaard said the inward turn risked “generational damage” to US diplomacy and global leadership.
“The scaling back of US forward presence shifts resources and attention to the western hemisphere and domestic missions, and reduces the US ability to deter adversaries globally. Nato and Indo-Pacific allies see US commitments as transactional and domestic-focused, encouraging them to explore defence autonomy,” Odgaard said.
“While it may strengthen short-term homeland security, it significantly reduces Washington’s global presence diplomatically and militarily, and this creates strategic vacuums that competitors like China and Russia are poised to exploit.”
The Trump administration has also been pressuring its allies to increase defence spending. Indo-Pacific countries such as Australia, Japan and South Korea have all been urged to spend 3.5 per cent of their gross domestic product on defence.
Barton said the US military was a “slightly different animal” to the administration, and it would be unlikely to “scrap preparedness for conflict with China at any point or to consider its containment a secondary priority”.
He said China would remain its “gravest strategy competitor”, adding: “Presidents come and go, but the threat levels remain the same.”
He said there was a difference between Trump’s first and second administrations and, since January, his general stance on China had been far more accommodating.

“The diplomatic bilateral tone on China might have become less incandescent under Trump 2.0, but I find it hard to imagine that he would want his administration to be known as being ready to back down on China and hand it a free rein over the Indo-Pacific,” Barton added.
He also pointed to accusations that Trump was “pandering” to China, for example by reportedly asking Japanese Prime Minister Sanae Takaichi to backtrack on her comments that an attack on Taiwan might trigger Japanese military intervention.
“Although I doubt the US would remain passive – even under Trump – in case of an attempted Chinese invasion of Taiwan, the current US position on this issue seems at its most ambiguous,” Barton said.
“Yes, it continues to sell arms to Taiwan, but would it want to immediately risk the lives of US soldiers? I’m unsure.”

data.title: Hong Kong police arrest man for allegedly vandalising Legco election posters
data.description:
Hong Kong police have arrested a 36-year-old man for allegedly vandalising Legislative Council election campaign materials ahead of Sunday’s poll.
Police said on Saturday that they were alerted at 8.53am that five Legco election-related posters on No 30 Mei Tin Road in Tai Wai had allegedly been destroyed.
After a preliminary probe, the suspect was arrested for alleged criminal damage and detained for investigation, police said.
The force’s Sha Tin division is following up on the case, according to police.
It is an offence to destroy or damage any property belonging to another without lawful excuse, and such actions carry a maximum penalty of 10 years in prison.
Police conducted multiple law enforcement actions in recent weeks to crack down on vandalism of election campaign materials.
Earlier last month, two men, aged 29 and 34, were arrested on suspicion of criminal damage after officers found three vandalised election campaign posters hanging from the Tong Mi Road footbridge in Mong Kok.
Also in November, three men were arrested in separate cases for allegedly damaging election campaign banners.
The election will see 51 candidates competing for the 20 seats in the 10 geographical constituencies where residents will cast their ballots.
Another 60 are vying for 30 seats in the functional constituencies, and the remaining 50 for 40 seats in the Election Committee constituency. These seats are decided by sector-specific voters.

data.title: China mum cares for daughter-in-law in vegetative state, borrows US$140,000 for treatment
data.description:
A woman in central China, who borrowed one million yuan (US$140,000) to treat her daughter-in-law, has captured the hearts of millions on social media through her unwavering dedication.
For the past five years, she has been providing round-the-clock care for her daughter-in-law, Yuanyuan, who fell into a vegetative state after being struck by a car on June 25, 2020.
Yuanyuan suffered severe injuries to her head, face, arms, and pelvis, along with multiple bone fractures. Following a craniotomy, she lapsed into a deep coma.

Fortunately, after four months, she regained a glimmer of consciousness, yet she remains in a condition akin to a vegetative state, unable to open her mouth, turn over, or dress herself. She also experiences incontinence, according to Henan TV.
Her mother-in-law, Liu Zhenyan, a resident of Xinxiang in Henan, never considered giving up on Yuanyuan. Instead, she has devoted herself to her care for the last five years.
During Yuanyuan’s first year in the hospital, Liu made a bed on the floor and slept beside her daughter-in-law’s bed each night. Liu’s exceptional care led other patients and their families to believe she was Yuanyuan’s mother rather than her mother-in-law.

Since Yuanyuan cannot eat on her own, Liu carefully opens her mouth to feed her, showing extraordinary patience. Additionally, she bathes Yuanyuan regularly, understanding her daughter-in-law’s love for cleanliness.
When necessary, Liu carries Yuanyuan on her back to move her around.
“When Yuanyuan was injured in the accident, doctors said there was only a one per cent chance of saving her,” Liu recalled.
“I remember rushing to the hospital, and hearing Yuanyuan call me ‘Mum’ reaffirmed my belief that I had a responsibility to care for her, no matter how challenging the future might be.”

Liu’s son and Yuanyuan, who were high school sweethearts, have been married for 15 years and have two children together.
Liu shared the immense challenge of borrowing money to cover the medical bills.
“At times when we couldn’t secure a loan, I wished it were me lying in bed instead of my daughter-in-law. I wanted my son, his wife, and their two children to live happily together,” she said.
Liu’s son has also remained committed to his wife.
“While Yuanyuan was in the hospital, I told my son that if she didn’t survive, I hoped he would never remarry because I didn’t want my grandchildren to have a stepmother. He agreed immediately,” Liu revealed.
Liu described the close relationship she previously shared with Yuanyuan: “She treated me like her mother, and I treated her like my daughter. This bond is a tradition in my family, as my own mother-in-law treated me well.”

Recently, Liu went to great lengths to fund a surgery aimed at repairing a section of Yuanyuan’s skull, which was severely damaged in the accident.
“I know Yuanyuan loves being beautiful,” Liu explained. “Since she cannot move independently, I am her crutch. I don’t know how long it will take for her to recover fully, but I am committed to taking care of her.”
The family’s story has gained significant attention on social media, amassing over 12 million views online.
“What a wonderful mother-in-law! She deserves to be honoured as a ‘Moving China Figure’,” commented one online observer.
“I am moved to tears. I hope the media will share more positive stories like this,” expressed another.

data.title: As technology reshapes modern warfare, Ukrainian women embrace combat roles
data.description:
When Russia’s full-scale invasion began nearly four years ago, a 26-year-old soldier known as Monka did not see a combat role she could do. But that changed as technology reshaped the battlefield and opened new paths.
Last year, she joined the military as a pilot of short-range, first-person view, or FPV, drones after giving up a job managing a restaurant abroad and returning home to Ukraine to serve.
Her shift is part of a larger trend of more women joining Ukraine’s military in combat roles, a change made possible by the technological transformation of modern warfare, military officials say.
“The fact that technology lets us deliver ammunition without carrying it in our hands or running it to the front line – that’s incredible,” said Monka, who serves in the Unmanned Systems Battalion of the Third Army Corps. She and other women followed Ukraine’s military protocol by identifying themselves using only their call signs.

More than 70,000 women served in Ukraine’s military in 2025, a 20 per cent increase compared to 2022, including over 5,500 deployed directly on the front line, according to Ukraine’s defence ministry.
Some units have tailored recruitment efforts towards women, expanding rosters in a sign that Ukraine is looking to strengthen and expand its army even as peace negotiations weigh a possible cap on the future size of the military.
Leaders in the capital Kyiv, as well as many soldiers like Monka, see the army as one of the few security guarantees that Ukraine has against Russia.
“We need everyone – engineers, pilots, IT specialists, programmers, we simply need brains. It’s not about men or women. We need people who are ready to work hard,” she said.
A drone pilot is one of the Ukrainian military’s most popular combat professions chosen by women, according to military officials.

When Imla from the Kraken 1654 unit left her career as a professional hockey player to join the military, the 27-year-old initially planned to become a combat paramedic.
She spent her first six months as a platoon doctor, but the job required learning to fly drones. She started with small ones before moving to larger models carrying bombs and eventually switching to full-time drone work.
Imla clearly remembers her first drone flight, a reconnaissance mission. When they handed her the controller, she was so nervous her hands wouldn’t stop shaking.
“To be honest, I even wanted to cry in some moments,” she recalled. “But then, over time, you build up experience on the job and start feeling confident.”
The Khartiia Corps has taken more women into its ranks, reporting a 20 per cent increase since 2024. About six months ago, the brigade launched a recruitment campaign aimed at women for combat and technological roles in cooperation with the Dignitas Foundation, a charity organisation supporting Ukraine by funding technological innovation and civic development projects.
“In recent months, dozens of women have joined us in combat roles and are working successfully,” said Volodymyr Dehtyarov, the Khartiia Corps public affairs officer. “The more technology we have, like drones, the more historically male professions open up.”

Khartiia has started training officers and future commanders on how to work with mixed units including people of different ages, genders and backgrounds.
Dehtyarov said this had helped commanders become more effective leaders.
The Ukrainian army remains conservative at its core and some units do not make it easy for women.
A 25-year-old soldier with the call sign Yaha joined the military in 2023 and initially did paperwork as an army clerk. Three months later, she began asking to attend drone courses. Commanders at the time did not respond with enthusiasm and instead suggested she replace the cook.
“It was unpleasant for me, because I didn’t expect such uncomfortable conditions, such strict limitations,” Yaha said.
In the kitchen, she spent her free time studying drone manuals, practising on a simulator and training in computer clubs with a controller she bought herself.
“I liked that you could strike the enemy remotely,” she said. “So I thought this was our future.”
Eventually, she became a bomber-drone pilot in the ninth brigade.
“War is not cool or glamorous. It’s pain, suffering and loss. You just do it because you want to change the situation,” she said. “But you’re not invincible. You’re just a person like everyone else.”

Chibi, a 20-year-old FPV technician from the Khartiia brigade, prepares drones for the battlefield from a dark, damp basement near the front line in eastern Ukraine.
She initially faced prejudice from soldiers who claimed she had inferior technical skills because she was a woman. But she also had a supportive male colleague who helped her take the first steps towards becoming an FPV technician, which she finds more interesting than being a pilot.
“There needs to be more women in the army,” Chibi said, her hair dyed pink and dark blue. “The more women there are, the better the attitude towards them will be.”
Olha Meloshyna, the spokeswoman for Ukraine’s Unmanned Systems Forces, says the belief that drone roles are safer is wrong, as Russians actively hunt drone operators.
Their unit is seeing more women move into technological roles, including drone operation, drone repair and electronic warfare, as drones have become one of the main tools of striking and reconnaissance on the battlefield.

According to Meloshyna, 4.2 per cent of the Unmanned Systems Forces are women, a number she considers significant because women enlist voluntarily.
“We are part of the new Ukrainian army that formed during the invasion. So in terms of gender-based acceptance into the armed forces, we have never had any division – what matters to us is desire and motivation,” she said, adding that they were now conducting a more media-focused recruitment campaign, inviting and planning to recruit 15,000 people to join, including women.
Recruiters say that women are applying for both combat and non-combat positions.
“The Unmanned Systems Forces are a system, and it is made up of people – men and women,” Meloshyna said. “No drone is autonomous. It needs human involvement. And the more personnel we have, the more drones will fly towards Russia.”

data.title: 10 hurt in collision between KMB bus, rubbish truck and taxi in Hong Kong
data.description:
Ten people were injured in a collision between a double-decker KMB bus, a rubbish truck and a taxi in Kwai Chung on Saturday.
Police said the drivers of all three vehicles and seven bus passengers sustained injuries.
According to the force, the incident occurred at 298 Kwai Shing Circuit and was reported to authorities by the bus driver at 2.57pm.

A police spokesman said that those hurt in the incident had sustained light injuries, with the driver of the rubbish truck taken to Yan Chai Hospital in Tsuen Wan for treatment.
Images of the scene shared online showed the double-decker bus severely damaged on its right side, with windows shattered and body panels ripped off.

data.title: K-drama casting news about Yuna of K-pop group Itzy, Yoo Yeon-seok, Jung So-min and more
data.description:
Another major K-pop name begins to build her K-drama résumé in this week’s Korean casting news, which includes a romantic drama, an office romcom, a supernatural legal show and an action-packed casino-set series.
1. My Favorite Employee
Yuna of the girl group Itzy has booked her second major drama role in the upcoming series My Favorite Employee. She will be joining series leads Kang Hoon (A Time Called You), Kim Hye-jun (A Shop for Killers) and Cha Woo-min (Melo Movie).
The series, which is based on a webtoon, will follow several characters working at a company launched by a former K-pop idol.

Kim will appear as Nam Da-reum, a major fan of idol Lee Chan (played by Cha), who begins working at his company. Kang stars as her talented boss Kang Ha-gi, and the story will focus on the romance that develops between them.
Yuna will appear as a top actress. Before this, she will make her acting debut with a supporting role in Undercover Miss Hong with Park Shin-hye, due out in January.
The 12-episode My Favorite Employee will be directed by Park Ji-hyun (Class of Lies) and is due out on tvN at some point next year.
2. Shin Yi-rang’s Law Firm
Yoo Yeon-seok (Hospital Playlist) and Esom (Taxi Driver) have been confirmed as the leads of a new legal drama called Shin Yi-rang’s Law Firm for broadcaster SBS.

Yoo has been tapped to play the titular Shin Yi-rang, a lawyer who opens a new firm in a building that was once home to a shaman. No sooner does he begin his practice than he starts seeing ghosts.
Despite his debonair appearance, Yi-rang is a shy lawyer, but he rises to the challenges posed by his new ghost clients, which include being possessed by their personalities.
Joining him on his unusual legal journey is Han Na-hyun (Esom), an ace lawyer at a top law firm who experiences an unexpected legal loss thanks to Yi-rang. Despite this rocky start, the two join forces.

In 2025, Yoo made special appearances in Tastefully Yours and Park Chan-wook’s acclaimed film No Other Choice, while Esom starred in LTNS and the Netflix film Mantis.
3. Dealer
Jung So-min (Alchemy of Souls) and Ryoo Seung-bum (Moving) are circling the casino-set drama Dealer, an upcoming Netflix production.
Jung is being eyed for the role of Jung Gun-hwa, a dealer at the Oceanland Casino who awakens dormant superpowers to save her upcoming wedding, only to find herself falling into a dangerous criminal underworld.

Ryoo has been offered the role of Hwang Chi-soo, who once had a family and a stable job, before squandering everything on the casino table. Now he works as a shill at Oceanland, the very establishment that cleaned him out.
For Jung, this role would mark a change after three well-received romcoms in a row: the film Love Reset, and the dramas Love Next Door and Would You Marry Me.
Ryoo is no stranger to casino stories, having headlined the film Tazza: One Eyed Jack. Recently, he appeared in the Netflix film Good News and the series Family Matters. He reportedly declined to reprise his role in a second season of the latter.
4. Another Love, Not You
Recently seen in Undercover High School, Seo Kang-joon will next appear in the show Another Love, Not You.

In this romantic drama that depicts the trials of long-term couples, Seo will play Namgoong Ho, an ordinary office worker whose partner of 10 years starts to cool towards him. Then he gets swept up in another emotional connection.
The series will follow four protagonists in similar circumstances, all of whom have been in relationships for over a decade, whose loves are tested when new people appear before them. For now, the other cast members have not been revealed.
Seo took a three-year gap between Grid and Undercover High School to complete his military service. However, he will feature in a cameo role in next year’s Netflix series Boyfriend on Demand with Blackpink’s Jisoo and Seo In-guk.

data.title: Probe links Philippines flood corruption to Pogo crypto schemes
data.description:
Funds siphoned off from the Philippines’ flood control projects and converted into cryptocurrency may be linked to online scam syndicates run by offshore gaming operators, an official from the country’s cybercrime unit has revealed.
Renato Paraiso, acting executive director of the Cybercrime Investigation and Coordinating Centre, told This Week in Asia that investigators had uncovered a “definite link” between digital assets tied to the multibillion-peso flood control scandal and cryptocurrency scams carried out by Philippine offshore gaming operators (Pogos).
President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr banned Pogos last year amid public outrage over their alleged involvement in criminal activities. The government has since intensified its crackdown, sentencing former Bamban mayor and Chinese national Alice Guo and seven others to life imprisonment for human trafficking connected to Pogo-linked syndicates.
A preliminary government investigation found that about 545 billion pesos (US$9 billion) had been spent on nearly 10,000 flood control projects since 2022, many of which were later discovered to be substandard or never built.
Lawmakers, public works officials and contractors are alleged to have colluded to siphon off large portions of those funds.


“There’s a definite link because these are the same exchanges or the same personalities that are converting USDT or cryptocurrency accounts for the people that we are suspecting are involved in Pogo operations, as well as these personalities involved in this flood control scandal,” Paraiso said.
He added that intelligence reports showed people linked to the flood control scheme were funnelling large sums into Tether (USDT), a stablecoin pegged to the US dollar.
The findings emerged after cyber investigators monitored unusual online activity requesting massive amounts of Tether, with transactions ranging from 50 million to 100 million pesos each.
To obscure the money trail, launderers allegedly moved the funds out of the Philippines and into various digital assets, including through “cold wallets” that store cryptocurrency offline.
Paraiso, who is leading recovery efforts, said these findings had been shared with a government working group focused on retrieving assets linked to the corruption scandal.
The Philippine Anti-Money Laundering Council had also reported freezing Tether accounts and other digital currencies tied to suspicious activities traced to individuals implicated in the scheme, Paraiso said.

Criminal ‘service for hire’?
The use of cryptocurrency to launder illicit funds followed patterns seen in other recent crimes, Paraiso said.
His agency has observed that digital assets have become “the currency of choice for criminals” – a trend made chillingly clear earlier this year by the case of Filipino-Chinese businessman Anson Que, who was kidnapped and murdered in April.
Que’s family had paid roughly 200 million pesos in ransom that was funnelled through e-wallets used for casino gambling and crypto conversions.
Police later linked the ransom laundering to casino junket operators 9 Dynasty Group and White Horse Club, both tied to Chinese nationals allegedly involved in Pogo-related scams.
Former Cybercrime Investigation and Coordinating Centre executive director Alexander Ramos told This Week in Asia that the agency had been tracking the flow of digital currency in cases of kidnapping, drug trafficking, online scams and extortion over the past three years.
“But we have not tackled issues of currencies that were being taken out because of corruption,” Ramos said. “Is it a possibility? Maybe.”
It could even be a “service for hire” being conducted from within the country, he added.
Ramos recalled that authorities seized large amounts of cash during the height of the Pogo crackdown. “These scam centres were converting their revenues into cash” and then into something else, he said. “One of the things we were looking at was cryptocurrency.”
He implored investigators to examine casinos as possible conduits for crypto laundering, citing reports that public works officials involved in the flood-control scandal converted their kickbacks into casino chips, cashed them out and deposited the proceeds as purported winnings.

Tether and the black market
Crypto experts said the launderers’ preference for Tether was unsurprising given its wide use across Southeast Asia.
“USDT is the oldest stablecoin and has the deepest liquidity,” said Luis Buenaventura, head of crypto at Philippine e-wallet GCash. “The larger the amounts we’re evaluating, the more likely it is that USDT would be the only token capable of absorbing that kind of buy volume locally.”
Laundering money through Tether was likely done through the black market, Buenaventura said, but launderers with sufficient access to multiple accounts could even conduct trades on legitimate exchanges without triggering alarms.
“There would indeed be a paper trail, but if you’re staying within platform limits, these activities can remain undetected for a while,” he told This Week in Asia.
Jiro Reyes, co-founder and CEO of the crypto education platform Bitskwela, said Tether dominated stablecoin circulation across the region.
“USDT is where the liquidity is, USDT is where the informal networks already operate,” Reyes said.
Launderers frequently exploit these networks by routing funds through multiple wallets, decentralised apps, foreign exchanges and offshore intermediaries.
The goal was to make everything so “fragmented” that it would be “outside the capability of our government to fully unwind”, Reyes said.
According to Ramos, the Philippines’ loosely regulated crypto environment provides cover for such activities.
“There are no safeguards, no tracking or reporting system ,” he said. “We are a country that conducts so many online transactions, yet we have insufficient safety nets.”

data.title: Inflated asset prices show markets don’t always know best
data.description:
Stock prices are nervously high, bond and real estate prices are under downward pressure, cryptocurrency assets have sustained falls and global debt is at record levels. One common factor behind these coinciding and worrying trends is that much of the world is living on excess credit. Payment is now due.
Another is how markets have bloated as financial systems fail to properly channel savings into good investments.
This all suggests the coming correction in financial markets will be more “shocking” (in all senses of that word) than previous cases. It is likely to result in a dramatic reset of financial regulation architecture.
Excess credit began in earnest following the collapse of the dotcom bubble when the prices of tech stocks on Wall Street crashed by nearly 80 per cent, scaring financial authorities into taking supposedly remedial monetary action to ward off feared economic recession as stock market wealth evaporated.
Recession was again bought off on a much more ambitious (dangerous) scale with the onset of the global financial crisis in the late 2000s, after which hundreds of billions of dollars of monetary and fiscal stimulus were injected into Western economies by central banks and governments. China also applied massive stimulus, although via policy-directed state institutions rather than simply throwing money into the market.
Interest rates were cut by central banks, led by the US Federal Reserve, to record lows. Central banks also referred to the Bank of Japan’s experience with quantitative monetary easing, which saw them purchase huge amounts of government bonds and private securities to boost financial liquidity. The aim was to get money flowing more freely and faster. A much more severe recession and mass unemployment were probably avoided or ameliorated by these actions.


However, problems were further compounded by the onset of the Covid-19 pandemic from 2019 onwards, which prompted some governments to make substantial cash payments to households to ward off recession. Economic activity inevitably suffered, however, and this had to be countered by other forms of fiscal stimulus.
The consequences of introducing so much new money into the economy, including through bank loans, have been severe. Financial markets are now reckoning with those consequences, which could have been avoided if markets had been left to find their own way. Of course, the social and political costs of that would have been huge.
Government and central bank money has been poured liberally into the system in the US and other market economies without any coordinated strategy on how and where it should be deployed to maximise economic and social benefit. The underlying assumption is that “the market knows best” and that it is the most efficient allocator of funds.
However, the evidence suggests otherwise. Yes, economic stimulus tends to raise personal consumption, which in turn increases demand for goods and services, prompting an increase in industrial output and invigorated economic growth. However, a very large part of the official stimulus is spent on acquiring financial assets. That creates the widespread asset inflation we are witnessing now.

Stocks and bonds are the easiest forms of financial assets to acquire in this regard, and securitisation of physical assets ranging from real estate to commodities has made access even easier, as has the dramatic growth of exchange-traded funds that allow retail access to multiple assets from stocks and shares to cryptocurrencies and financial derivatives.
This accelerating “democratisation” of finance and monetisation of economies has led to the blowout taking place across a broad spectrum of asset markets. Apart from the tenuous sustainability of this approach, the key question is whether it results in an efficient allocation of financial resources.
Clearly, it does not, as is obvious from the fact that sectors of the equity market such as tech and artificial intelligence stocks are experiencing hyperinflation as a result of obsessive attention by speculative investors – supposedly responsible financial institutions, not just retail investors – while basic industries, infrastructure, climate change alleviation and other areas go underfunded.
Why do governments, financial institutions and individuals put so much faith in markets when they are constantly prone to repeated boom-and-bust episodes, and are unable to allocate funds to areas of social and economic importance where they are most needed?

Those commentators who seek to warn us of this unsustainable trend are often labelled “doom-mongers”, whereas the onus should really be on free-market champions to show that repeated asset bubbles and market crises are an essential part of the price to be paid for growth and economic progress.
Every time financial asset prices surge to bubble highs, status quo defenders seem to argue that “this time it’s different” and that markets are heading towards a bright and shining future of exponential growth that must be paid for by the economic hardship of the present.
This time is indeed different, although not for the reasons claimed by market boosters whose “irrational exuberance” would make even Voltaire’s excessively optimistic character Pangloss from Candide blush. It is different because so many market bubbles are being inflated at the same time. When one of them bursts, others are likely to pop from the contagion effect.
The impact promises to be disastrous, enough perhaps to trigger shifts from market-driven to state-steered economies, as well as prompting a corresponding shift in the regulation of savings and investments in which the state becomes more important in influencing the overall flow of funds through these economies.

data.title: Eileen Gu returns to scene of her Beijing Olympic triumphs – and wins again
data.description:
Eileen Gu returned to the mountain where she enjoyed great success at the Beijing Olympics to capture the first Snow League title for freeskiing on Saturday.
Gu, the 22-year-old who won two golds and a silver at the 2022 Games, won all six of her head-to-head showdowns against other skiers whose runs in this new format are judged against each other in a set of best-of-three contests through three rounds.
For winning the first skiing event in Shaun White’s new half-pipe league, Gu takes home a US$50,000 first prize.
“I’m so happy we got to do the very first freeski Snow League here in China. It’s so special to share the podium with two of my teammates, as well,” Gu said.
Gu was part of an all-China podium, with Zhang Kexin finishing second and Li Fanghui in third.
Part of Gu’s motivation to ski for China was to get more girls in the country involved in her sport – an effort she said was having an impact.
“For any pioneer of anything, the most important thing is that the people who follow will have the road a little bit easier than they had it,” Gu said. “My biggest hope is that any girl in the park, no matter what she looks like, where she’s from, how old she is, she never feels like she doesn’t belong.”
Next year in Italy at the Winter Olympics, Gu will be a favourite in all three freeski disciplines – half-pipe, slopestyle and big air.
Three years ago in China, she won gold in half-pipe and big air and a silver in slopestyle.
Gu will return to the same Secret Garden snow park in Zhangjiakou next week for a World Cup event.
Her first event at the Olympics is scheduled for February 7.
In Friday’s snowboard contests, Olympic champion Ayumu Hirano of Japan won the men’s event and 14-year-old Patti Zhou of China won the women’s.

data.title: Hong Kong contractors race to remove scaffolding nets after Tai Po fire
data.description:
Contractors in Hong Kong are racing to tear down scaffolding nets used for estate renovation works, with some applying for extensions from authorities as the government’s end-Saturday deadline looms.
An industry representative on Saturday estimated that work at all sites across the city would only be completed by early next week, given the tight deadline, lengthy processes and a limited number of trained workers in the city.
The development authority announced the removal order for all construction sites in Hong Kong on Wednesday, following a police investigation into a Shandong-based manufacturer that allegedly falsified safety certificates for scaffolding netting used in at least two residential sites.
Among those asking for an extension is Sui Wo Court in Fo Tan, which has more than 3,500 flats, while the contractor of Elegance Garden in Tuen Mun, affected by a brief strike earlier, may also need more time.
“Most of the scaffolding companies, or supervisors we spoke to, told us they are not able to complete the tearing down today or tomorrow,” said Ho Ping-tak, chairman of the Hong Kong and Kowloon Bamboo Scaffolding Workers Union.
“For example, one building is about 30 storeys high and only has seven workers. They have been working for three days and they are still not done, just for one building.”
He estimated that workers would need until next Tuesday or Wednesday to remove the scaffolding net at about 200 locations across Hong Kong.
Fire danger imposed by non-fire-retardant protective nets has sparked public concerns after the Tai Po inferno killed 159 people.
Ho said other than tearing down the scaffolding nets, workers also have to clear equipment and construction material placed on the building’s exterior, which were now left exposed and could fall and injure pedestrians.
“It is impossible to only remove the net and ignore the other parts of the procedure.”
Ho said his union was not consulted about the timeline.

At Sui Wo Court in Fo Tan, its contractor Fulam Construction Engineering has applied to extend the deadline until Sunday, according to the Housing Bureau.
“As of [Friday], the contractor has already removed 70 per cent of the nets,” a bureau spokesman said.
The estate has nine towers with “extensive exterior wall area”, said the bureau, and the contractor estimates the removal will be done by December 7.
But a representative from Sui Wo Court’s management office told the Post on Saturday that the workers were working “as fast as they can” to complete the removal by Monday at the earliest.
Alfred Ng, who has lived with his family of three in the estate for two years, said the renovation project had been repeatedly delayed.
“It would be more reassuring if it [mesh] could be removed on time,” he said.
He added the original plan was to remove all the scaffolding by February 14 next year, but with the early dismantling, he had “no idea” when renovation would resume.
Tse Sui-fong, 70, a resident of the estate, said 40 per cent of the renovation work remained unfinished, even though the project had an initial target for completion by June this year.
“We have all been anxious and scared for almost three years,” she said, adding that the scaffolding and nets were put up outside the building in October 2023.
The scaffolding of her Block F, where she lives, was supposed to be removed on Saturday, but she was told everything would be removed by next Thursday.
“I told the management office that I am very scared. We can’t sleep at night. Why haven’t you started the removal?”

Chau Sze-kit, chairman of the Hong Kong Construction Industry Employees General Union, agreed that manpower and the tight deadline posed a challenge.
“We have some 2,000 to 3,000 scaffolding workers in Hong Kong and if we set aside everything else, we can remove all scaffolding in three days. But the reality is that we have other tasks in parallel, which makes it difficult to arrange manpower,” he said.
Chau also pointed out that workers at Elegance Garden had briefly gone on strike on Thursday, making it more difficult to meet the deadline. Workers had said that they were owed wages, drawing labour authorities and district officers on site to mediate.
But Mark Chong Ho-fung, a member of the Tuen Mun area committee, said that the scaffolding subcontractor eventually came to a consensus with the estate’s owners’ corporation and resumed work.
The company did not apply for an extension with housing authorities as they were making good progress, he said.
“They have started tearing down since yesterday and will be doing their best to complete the removal by the end of today,” he said.
Chong, who is a member of the centrist Roundtable party and running for the Legislative Council election on Sunday, said his party was in talks with housing authorities to give workers some flexibility in case they were unable to complete it by the end of the day.
The government said on Friday night that as of 3pm on the day, 115 private buildings had removed their scaffolding nets from the external walls. That number accounts for roughly half of the around 200 locations subject to the removal order.
Ho said that after the rush to tear down the net, the industry would face weeks without work as projects came to a standstill.
“Authorities will take time to test the net, and we do not know how long is needed to find and procure material that fulfils their fire safety requirements,” he said.
“Once the net is removed, all other works like exterior renovation and plumbing cannot be carried out, leading to unemployment in other fields.”
To address concerns from scaffolding workers facing a hiatus, the Construction Industry Council on Friday announced that it would offer 5,000 workers to enrol in courses, where they would receive up to HK$19,000 (US$2,440) in subsidies upon completion.
Additional reporting by Leopold Chen
